name: Southern Min (Minnan)

ancestors.notes: Min split from the Sinitic mainstream before the Middle Chinese of the rime dictionaries; its colloquial layer is not derivable from Middle Chinese (Norman 1988).
Middle Chinese supplies Minnan's literary reading layer (文讀) alongside the older colloquial layer.

notes: Grapheme inventory NOT YET SPECIFIED (stub). Southern Min (Hokkien / Taiwanese / Teochew) is written with Chinese characters (Han script), and also — uniquely among the Sinitic varieties — with a long-established full Latin orthography. No logographic character-to-phoneme map is claimed here: Chinese characters do not encode pronunciation systematically, so a Han-script G2P for this variety would need either a full character-reading lexicon or a romanised input. INPUT CONTRACT: this spec expects a Minnan romanisation as input, not Han characters — Pe̍h-ōe-jī (POJ, the missionary church romanisation) or the official Taiwanese Romanization System (Tâi-lô, 臺灣閩南語羅馬字拼音方案), which are inter-convertible. Compare: `zh` expects Hanyu Pinyin, Cantonese expects Jyutping, Hakka expects Pha̍k-fa-sṳ, Mindong expects Bàng-uâ-cê, Wu expects Wugniu. Minnan has 7-8 tones with pervasive sandhi; tone is written by POJ/Tâi-lô diacritics or digits. A POJ/Tâi-lô grapheme map is the obvious route to promote this spec to Tier A. PHONEME INVENTORY: attested inventory from PHOIBLE (glottocode minn1241), declared directly. `graphemes` stays empty because none of that depends on the writing system: the sounds of this language are known regardless of whether — or how — it is written.
